name: Dark Pool on Solana
description: A dark pool DEX where trade sizes and counterparties remain hidden until settlement, using Arcium MPC to match orders without revealing them to any single party (including the protocol itself).
possible_solution: Build a sealed-order matching engine using Arcium circuits. Orders are submitted encrypted with Enc<Shared, T>; the circuit compares bids/asks and executes matches; results are revealed only to matched parties via .reveal() after settlement. Reference the sealed_bid_auction example in arcium-hq/examples as a starting point for encrypted comparison logic.
resources:
1. Arcium examples: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples
2. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/dark_pool.md
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers
article_content:
Problem to Solve:

A dark pool DEX where trade sizes and counterparties remain hidden until settlement, using Arcium MPC to match orders without revealing them to any single party (including the protocol itself).

Possible Solution:

Orders are submitted encrypted with Enc<Shared, T>; the circuit compares bids/asks and executes matches; results are revealed only to matched parties via .reveal() after settlement. Reference the sealed_bid_auction example in arcium-hq/examples as a starting point for encrypted comparison logic.

author: Arcium

name: Sealed-Bid Auction
description: An on-chain sealed-bid auction where bids remain hidden from all parties — including the auctioneer — until the auction closes. Everyone can verify the winner is correct without ever seeing the losing bids.
possible_solution: Use Arcium's persistent encrypted state pattern. Bids submitted as Enc<Shared, Bid>; auction state accumulated in Enc<Mxe, AuctionState> tracking highest_bid, highest_bidder, and second_highest_bid; winner revealed via .reveal() after close. Full working example exists in arcium-hq/examples/sealed_bid_auction.
resources:
1. Sealed bid auction example: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples/tree/main/sealed_bid_auction
2. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/sealed_bid_auctions.md
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers

name: Encrypted On-Chain Voting
description: A private voting system where individual votes are hidden but the aggregate result is publicly verifiable — no trusted tallier needed, no single party can see who voted for what.
possible_solution: Use Arcium's persistent state pattern. Each voter submits Enc<Shared, UserVote>; circuit accumulates into Enc<Mxe, VoteStats> as yes/no counters; result revealed at end via .reveal() on the winning side. Full working example exists in arcium-hq/examples/voting — a great starting point for governance or permissionless prediction market resolution.
resources:
1. Voting example: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples/tree/main/voting
2. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/confidential_voting_for_prediction_market_resolution.md
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers

name: On-Chain Encrypted Poker
description: A fully on-chain poker game where cards are dealt and managed using Arcium MPC, so no party — including the dealer — can see other players' hands. Solves the long-standing 'mental poker' problem on-chain.
possible_solution: Combine Arcium's ArcisRNG for deck shuffling, Pack<T> for efficient card storage (~26x compression for byte arrays), and multi-party input patterns for each player's moves. State machine governs game phases (deal, betting rounds, showdown). Reference the blackjack example in arcium-hq/examples as a starting point — poker adds multi-party hands, betting rounds, and eventual card reveal at showdown.
resources:
1. Blackjack example: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples/tree/main/blackjack
2. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/poker.md
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers

name: Confidential AI on Sensitive Data
description: Run AI model inference where the input data — user health records, financial data, private documents — is never exposed to the model operator or any other party. Users get useful AI output without giving up their data.
possible_solution: Encode model weights as Enc<Mxe, T> and user input as Enc<Shared, T>. Circuit performs dot products and activations inside MPC — prefer arithmetic over comparisons (much cheaper in MPC). Use fixed-size arrays for all tensors (no Vec/dynamic shapes). Reference share_medical_records example in arcium-hq/examples for handling sensitive encrypted user data patterns.
resources:
1. Share medical records example: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples/tree/main/share_medical_records
2. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/confidential_ai_on_sensitive_data.md
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers
article_content:
Problem to Solve:

Run AI model inference where the input data — user health records, financial data, private documents — is never exposed to the model operator or any other party. Users get useful AI output without giving up their data.

Possible Solution:

Encode model weights as Enc<Mxe, T> and user input as Enc<Shared, T>. Circuit performs dot products and activations inside MPC — prefer arithmetic over comparisons (much cheaper in MPC). Use fixed-size arrays for all tensors (no Vec/dynamic shapes). Reference share_medical_records example in arcium-hq/examples for handling sensitive encrypted user data patterns.

name: Confidential Matchmaking
description: A matchmaking protocol where user preference data — dating profiles, job skill sets, trading counterparty requirements — is matched without exposing the raw preferences to either party or the platform.
possible_solution: Encode preferences as Enc<Shared, T>; circuit computes compatibility score in Enc<Mxe, T>; reveal only the match result (boolean or score band), not the underlying preference data. Use SerializedSolanaPublicKey for party identity in circuits. Pack preference arrays with Pack<T> for efficiency. Guard any division against secret zero (both branches always execute in MPC).
resources:
1. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/confidential_matchmaking.md
2. Arcium examples: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples
3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers
article_content:
Problem to Solve:

A matchmaking protocol where user preference data — dating profiles, job skill sets, trading counterparty requirements — is matched without exposing the raw preferences to either party or the platform.

Possible Solution:

Encode preferences as Enc<Shared, T>; circuit computes compatibility score in Enc<Mxe, T>; reveal only the match result (boolean or score band), not the underlying preference data. Use SerializedSolanaPublicKey for party identity in circuits. Pack preference arrays with Pack<T> for efficiency. Guard any division against secret zero (both branches always execute in MPC).

Resources:

1. Arcium ideas list: https://github.com/arcium-hq/ideas-list/blob/main/ideas/confidential_matchmaking.md 2. Arcium examples: https://github.com/arcium-hq/examples 3. Arcium docs: https://docs.arcium.com/developers
